---
title: "Introduction to R"
subtitle: "For the R-Curious"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, styles.scss]
    self-contained: true
    chalkboard: false
    standalone: true
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: png
    dev.args: { bg: "transparent" }
---

```{r}
#| messages: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
```


## Who are we?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Dr. Elizabeth King** (she/her)

- Grew up in Southeastern Wisconsin (until 7th grade) $\rightarrow$ Chicago-land
- Iowa (4 years) $\rightarrow$ California (10 years) $\rightarrow$ Missouri
- MU 12 years in Biology
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Dr. Kevin Middleton** (he/him)

- Grew up in Florida
- North Carolina $\rightarrow$ Rhode Island (10 years) $\rightarrow$ California (1 year) $\rightarrow$ Rhode Island (1 year) $\rightarrow$ California (5 years) $\rightarrow$ Missouri
- MU 14 years (10 in Medicine, 4 in Biology)

:::

::::

<hr />

. . .

- Biologists by training
- Engineers, theoreticians, statisticians, and programmers by avocation
- We are married.


## A Bit of History



## Why are we here?

- Programming/data skills are increasingly important for you as researchers, regardless of field
- Making research more reproducible benefits all of us
- We have a strong interest in empowering trainees to gain data science skills
- You might find that you really enjoy it


## Why are you here?


## Outline

- Example use cases
- Introduction to R/RStudio & Reproducibility
- Roadmaps for different levels of learners
- Roadmaps for different specific skills


# Use Cases


## Creating a data sheet (EGK)

```{r}
#| echo: true

reps <- paste0("R", 1:3)
treats <- c("C", "DR", "HS")
lines <- c(
  paste(1:12, "DA", sep = "."),
  paste(1:12, "FA", sep = "."),
  paste(1:12, "HS", sep = ".")
)
dat.tab <- crossing(lines, treats, reps) |>
  arrange(reps)
box.r <- tibble(
  box = rep(paste0("box_", 1:4), each = 100),
  x = rep(rep(1:10, each = 10), 4),
  y = rep(rep(1:10, 10), 4)
)
box.r <- box.r[
  c(sample(1:100), sample(101:200), sample(201:300), sample(301:400)),
]
dat.tab <- cbind(dat.tab, box.r[1:nrow(dat.tab), ])
write_csv(dat.tab, "UseCase_datatable1.csv")

dat.tab |>
  arrange(box, x, y) |>
  write_csv("UseCase_datatable2.csv")

```

## Joining & pivoting (EGK)

- note - one ds for set up, one for data in cols
- join on set up date
- pivot on cols

## Exploratory Data Analysis

```{r}
ds <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/QM-Life-Sci/RDABR/refs/heads/main/data/Datasaurus_data.csv",
  col_names = c("x", "y"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(head(ds))
```

Correlation test:

```{r}
cor.test(ds$x, ds$y)
```


## Exploratory Data Analysis

```{r}
#| fig-alt: "Scatterplot that looks like a dinosaur."

ggplot(ds, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(color = "navy") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(
    caption = expression(paste("Alberto Cairo's ", italic("Datasaurus")))
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```


## Finding Errors (KMM)

## Doing the same thing to lots of things (EGK)


## Making beautiful figures (KMM)

![](https://cdn.fosstodon.org/media_attachments/files/116/209/557/460/653/062/original/ce83839bc0622920.png)


## Stats (the hard way now is the easy way later) & redoing stats & analysis reports (EGK)


## Reproducible instructions & code (KMM)


## Slides & presentations & papers & websites & CV (KMM)


## Introduction to R/RStudio & Reproducibility

- R is a general purpose programming language (that is optimized for statistics and data visualization)
- You can do all of these things in other languages (some easier and many more difficult)
- R enforces an "ask for what you want" statistical approach


# Roadmaps for different levels of learners

## What is your current skill level/experience?

## Roadmaps for different levels of learners
QR code for each then slide with grid of instructions

# Roadmaps for different specific skills

## What do you most want to learn/get better at?




## Slide 2

[Quarto]{.blue} enables you to weave together [content]{.yellow} and [executable code]{.yellow} into a **finished presentation**. To learn more about [Quarto]{.blue} presentations see <https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/>.

## Fenced divs

::: blue
Some paragraph.
:::

or

::: {.yellow}
Some paragraph.
:::

## Links

https://slidecrafting-book.com/scss
